verses.text: So the couriers passed from city to city through the country of Ephraim and Manasseh, even to Zebulun, but people ridiculed them and mocked them.
verses.explainer: The couriers pass from city to city through Ephraim, Manasseh, and as far as Zebulun, but the people laugh at and mock them.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The northern kingdom had its own worship system centered on Bethel and Dan. Many northerners would have seen Jerusalem's invitation as a political power play rather than genuine spiritual concern.

verses.text: Nevertheless some men of Asher and Manasseh and of Zebulun humbled themselves, and came to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Nevertheless, some from Asher, Manasseh, and Zebulun humble themselves and come to Jerusalem.

verses.text: Also the hand of God came on Judah to give them one heart, to do the commandment of the king and of the princes by Yahweh’s word.
verses.explainer: In Judah, the hand of God gives the people a united heart to carry out the command of the king and officials according to the word of the LORD.

verses.text: Many people assembled at Jerusalem to keep the feast of unleavened bread in the second month, a very great assembly.
verses.explainer: A very large assembly gathers in Jerusalem to celebrate the Feast of Unleavened Bread in the second month.

verses.text: They arose and took away the altars that were in Jerusalem, and they took away all the altars for incense and threw them into the brook Kidron.
verses.explainer: They remove the pagan altars in Jerusalem and the incense altars, throwing them into the Kidron Valley.

crossReferences.connection: Ahaz had set up altars on every corner of Jerusalem; now the people tear them down before Passover.

verses.text: Then they killed the Passover on the fourteenth day of the second month. The priests and the Levites were ashamed, and sanctified themselves, and brought burnt offerings into Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: They slaughter the Passover lamb on the fourteenth day of the second month. The priests and Levites are ashamed, consecrate themselves, and bring burnt offerings to the house of the LORD.

thenVsNow.then: When ordinary people show more spiritual passion than religious leaders, it can either shame leaders into action or expose institutional complacency. Here it motivated positive change.
thenVsNow.now: When ordinary people show more spiritual passion than religious leaders, it can either shame leaders into action or expose institutional complacency. Here it motivated positive change.

verses.text: They stood in their place after their order, according to the law of Moses the man of God. The priests sprinkled the blood which they received of the hand of the Levites.
verses.explainer: They stand in their places according to the Law of Moses, and the priests sprinkle the blood they receive from the Levites.

verses.text: For there were many in the assembly who had not sanctified themselves: therefore the Levites were in charge of killing the Passovers for everyone who was not clean, to sanctify them to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Many in the assembly have not consecrated themselves, so the Levites slaughter the Passover lambs for everyone who is not clean enough to consecrate their offering to the LORD.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Normally each family head killed their own Passover lamb. The Levites stepping in for those ritually unclean was a practical adaptation that prioritized participation over exclusion.

verses.text: For a multitude of the people, even many of Ephraim, Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun, had not cleansed themselves, yet they ate the Passover other than the way it is written. For Hezekiah had prayed for them, saying, “May the good Yahweh pardon everyone
verses.explainer: Many people from Ephraim, Manasseh, Issachar, and Zebulun have not purified themselves yet eat the Passover contrary to what is written. But Hezekiah prays for them:

verses.text: who sets his heart to seek God, Yahweh, the God of his fathers, even if they aren’t clean according to the purification of the sanctuary.”
verses.explainer: 'May the good LORD pardon everyone who sets their heart to seek God -- the LORD, the God of their fathers -- even though they are not cleansed according to the sanctuary's purification rules.'

thenVsNow.then: Hezekiah's prayer that God accept imperfectly prepared but sincere worshipers remains profoundly relevant -- God values the heart's intention over ritual perfection.
thenVsNow.now: Hezekiah's prayer that God accept imperfectly prepared but sincere worshipers remains profoundly relevant -- God values the heart's intention over ritual perfection.

verses.text: Yahweh listened to Hezekiah, and healed the people.
verses.explainer: The LORD hears Hezekiah and heals the people -- God responds to the prayer by accepting their imperfect worship.

crossReferences.connection: This is the fulfillment of God's promise: 'If my people humble themselves and seek my face... I will heal their land.'

verses.text: The children of Israel who were present at Jerusalem kept the feast of unleavened bread seven days with great gladness. The Levites and the priests praised Yahweh day by day, singing with loud instruments to Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The people of Israel present in Jerusalem celebrate the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days with great joy, with Levites and priests praising the LORD every day with loud instruments.

thenVsNow.then: Seven days of joyful worship with full musical accompaniment shows that genuine spiritual renewal is not somber but celebratory -- restoration produces joy, not just relief.
thenVsNow.now: Seven days of joyful worship with full musical accompaniment shows that genuine spiritual renewal is not somber but celebratory -- restoration produces joy, not just relief.

verses.text: Hezekiah spoke encouragingly to all the Levites who had good understanding in the service of Yahweh. So they ate throughout the feast for the seven days, offering sacrifices of peace offerings, and making confession to Yahweh, the God of their fathers.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah speaks encouragingly to all the Levites who show good skill in the service of the LORD. They eat the festival food for seven days, sacrificing peace offerings and giving thanks.

verses.text: The whole assembly took counsel to keep another seven days, and they kept another seven days with gladness.
verses.explainer: The whole assembly agrees to celebrate seven more days, and they celebrate another seven days with gladness.

verses.text: For Hezekiah king of Judah gave to the assembly for offerings one thousand bulls and seven thousand sheep; and the princes gave to the assembly a thousand bulls and ten thousand sheep: and a great number of priests sanctified themselves.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah contributes a thousand bulls and seven thousand sheep for the assembly. The officials contribute a thousand bulls and ten thousand sheep. A great number of priests consecrate themselves.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Royal and official contributions to the extended festival showed that the leadership was willing to bear significant cost for national worship -- putting their wealth where their faith was.

verses.text: All the assembly of Judah, with the priests and the Levites, and all the assembly who came out of Israel, and the foreigners who came out of the land of Israel, and who lived in Judah, rejoiced.
verses.explainer: The whole assembly of Judah rejoices, along with the priests, Levites, all who came from Israel, resident aliens from Israel, and those living in Judah.

verses.text: So there was great joy in Jerusalem; for since the time of Solomon the son of David king of Israel there was nothing like this in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: There is great joy in Jerusalem, for since the days of Solomon son of David king of Israel there has been nothing like this in Jerusalem.

crossReferences.connection: Solomon's original temple dedication festival, the only previous celebration comparable to Hezekiah's Passover.

verses.text: Then the priests the Levites arose and blessed the people. Their voice was heard, and their prayer came up to his holy habitation, even to heaven.
verses.explainer: The priests and Levites stand and bless the people. Their voice is heard, and their prayer comes to God's holy dwelling place in heaven.

thenVsNow.then: The assurance that prayer 'reaches heaven' -- that worship is not merely a human activity but genuinely connects with God -- is the foundation of all worship, then and now.
thenVsNow.now: The assurance that prayer 'reaches heaven' -- that worship is not merely a human activity but genuinely connects with God -- is the foundation of all worship, then and now.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Passover -- from the verb 'to pass over,' commemor
wordStudies.meaning: Passover -- from the verb 'to pass over,' commemorating God's passing over Israelite homes during the tenth plague in Egypt. Hezekiah's Passover reconnected the nation with its founding story.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To turn, return, repent -- the central verb of Hez
wordStudies.meaning: To turn, return, repent -- the central verb of Hezekiah's invitation to the northern tribes. Biblical repentance is not just feeling sorry but physically turning one's life direction back toward God.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To heal -- when God 'healed' the people who ate Pa
wordStudies.meaning: To heal -- when God 'healed' the people who ate Passover in ritual impurity, the word implies both physical restoration and spiritual acceptance. The same word appears in 2 Chronicles 7:14's promise.

explainer.summary: After the great Passover, the worshipers destroy pagan sites throughout Israel and Judah. Hezekiah then reorganizes the priestly and Levitical divisions, contributes from his own flocks for regular offerings, and commands the people to give the portion due to priests and Levites. The response is so generous that contributions pile up in heaps from the third month to the seventh month. Hezekiah appoints officials to distribute the surplus faithfully. The chapter shows that genuine worship revival requires sustained institutional support.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 31 shows that Hezekiah's reform was not just an emotional event but a structural reorganization. Sustainable worship requires systems for supporting those who lead it.

verses.text: Now when all this was finished, all Israel who were present went out to the cities of Judah, and broke the pillars in pieces, cut down the Asherah poles, and broke down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin, also in Ephraim and Manasseh, until they had destroyed them all. Then all the children of Israel returned, every man to his possession, into their own cities.
verses.explainer: When all this is finished, the Israelites present go out to the cities of Judah, smashing the sacred pillars, cutting down Asherah poles, and tearing down high places and altars throughout Judah, Benjamin, Ephraim, and Manasseh until they destroy them all.

thenVsNow.then: The people's spontaneous destruction of pagan sites shows that genuine worship renewal naturally leads to removing what competes with God -- revival is not just adding good practices but eliminating corrupting ones.
thenVsNow.now: The people's spontaneous destruction of pagan sites shows that genuine worship renewal naturally leads to removing what competes with God -- revival is not just adding good practices but eliminating corrupting ones.

verses.text: Hezekiah appointed the divisions of the priests and the Levites after their divisions, every man according to his service, both the priests and the Levites, for burnt offerings and for peace offerings, to minister, to give thanks, and to praise in the gates of Yahweh’s camp.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah appoints the divisions of the priests and Levites by their divisions, each according to their service, for burnt offerings and peace offerings, to minister, give thanks, and praise at the gates of the LORD's camp.

crossReferences.connection: David's original organization of priestly and Levitical divisions that Hezekiah now restores.

verses.text: He also appointed the king’s portion of his possessions for the burnt offerings, for the morning and evening burnt offerings, and the burnt offerings for the Sabbaths, for the new moons, and for the set feasts, as it is written in Yahweh’s law.
verses.explainer: The king contributes from his own possessions for the morning and evening burnt offerings, and for the burnt offerings on Sabbaths, new moons, and appointed festivals as written in the Law of the LORD.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The daily, weekly, and festival offerings required a constant supply of animals and grain. By funding these from his own wealth, Hezekiah ensured the worship calendar could function without burdening the people.

verses.text: Moreover he commanded the people who lived in Jerusalem to give the portion of the priests and the Levites, that they might give themselves to Yahweh’s law.
verses.explainer: He commands the people living in Jerusalem to give the portion due to the priests and Levites so they can devote themselves to the Law of the LORD.

thenVsNow.then: Supporting those who serve in ministry so they can 'devote themselves' to their calling remains a biblical principle -- those who serve God's people need material support to focus on their work.
thenVsNow.now: Supporting those who serve in ministry so they can 'devote themselves' to their calling remains a biblical principle -- those who serve God's people need material support to focus on their work.

verses.text: As soon as the commandment went out, the children of Israel gave in abundance the first fruits of grain, new wine, oil, honey, and of all the increase of the field; and they brought in the tithe of all things abundantly.
verses.explainer: As soon as the order goes out, the Israelites generously give the firstfruits of grain, wine, oil, honey, and all the produce of the field, and bring in abundantly the tithe of everything.

verses.text: The children of Israel and Judah, who lived in the cities of Judah, also brought in the tithe of cattle and sheep, and the tithe of dedicated things which were consecrated to Yahweh their God, and laid them in heaps.
verses.explainer: The people of Israel and Judah living in the cities of Judah also bring in the tithe of cattle and sheep, and the tithe of dedicated things consecrated to the LORD their God, laying them in heaps.

verses.text: In the third month they began to lay the foundation of the heaps, and finished them in the seventh month.
verses.explainer: They begin piling up the heaps in the third month and finish in the seventh month -- four months of continuous giving.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The third month through the seventh month covered the wheat harvest through the fruit harvest -- the people gave tithes from every harvest cycle, showing sustained commitment.

verses.text: When Hezekiah and the princes came and saw the heaps, they blessed Yahweh and his people Israel.
verses.explainer: When Hezekiah and the officials come and see the heaps, they bless the LORD and His people Israel.

thenVsNow.then: Leaders who see generous giving should respond as Hezekiah did -- with praise to God and gratitude toward the people, recognizing that generosity is itself a sign of spiritual health.
thenVsNow.now: Leaders who see generous giving should respond as Hezekiah did -- with praise to God and gratitude toward the people, recognizing that generosity is itself a sign of spiritual health.

verses.text: Then Hezekiah questioned the priests and the Levites about the heaps.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah asks the priests and Levites about the heaps.

verses.text: Azariah the chief priest, of the house of Zadok, answered him and said, “Since people began to bring the offerings into Yahweh’s house, we have eaten and had enough, and have plenty left over, for Yahweh has blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store.”
verses.explainer: Azariah the chief priest of the house of Zadok answers: 'Since the people began bringing their contributions to the house of the LORD, we have had enough to eat with plenty left over, for the LORD has blessed His people, and what is left is this great abundance.'

verses.text: Then Hezekiah commanded them to prepare rooms in Yahweh’s house, and they prepared them.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah orders storage rooms prepared in the house of the LORD, and they faithfully bring in the contributions, tithes, and dedicated things.

verses.text: They brought in the offerings, the tithes, and the dedicated things faithfully. Conaniah the Levite was ruler over them, and Shimei his brother was second.
verses.explainer: Conaniah the Levite is the officer in charge, with his brother Shimei as second. They faithfully oversee the contributions.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Appointing specific administrators for contributions ensured accountability and prevented corruption -- a principle of financial stewardship that applies to any institution.

verses.text: Jehiel, Azaziah, Nahath, Asahel, Jerimoth, Jozabad, Eliel, Ismachiah, Mahath, and Benaiah were overseers under the hand of Conaniah and Shimei his brother, by the appointment of Hezekiah the king and Azariah the ruler of God’s house.
verses.explainer: Jehiel, Azaziah, Nahath, Asahel, Jerimoth, Jozabad, Eliel, Ismachiah, Mahath, and Benaiah are appointed as supervisors under Conaniah and Shimei by order of King Hezekiah and Azariah the chief officer of the house of God.

verses.text: Kore the son of Imnah the Levite, the gatekeeper at the east gate, was over the freewill offerings of God, to distribute Yahweh’s offerings and the most holy things.
verses.explainer: Kore son of Imnah the Levite, keeper of the East Gate, is over the freewill offerings to God, distributing the contributions to the LORD and the most holy things.

verses.text: Under him were Eden, Miniamin, Jeshua, Shemaiah, Amariah, and Shecaniah, in the cities of the priests, in their office of trust, to give to their brothers by divisions, to the great as well as to the small;
verses.explainer: Under Kore are Eden, Miniamin, Jeshua, Shemaiah, Amariah, and Shecaniah in the cities of the priests, faithfully distributing to their brothers by divisions, great and small alike.

verses.text: besides those who were listed by genealogy of males, from three years old and upward, even everyone who entered into Yahweh’s house, as the duty of every day required, for their service in their offices according to their divisions;
verses.explainer: Distribution goes to males three years old and up who are registered by genealogy -- everyone who enters the house of the LORD for daily duties according to their divisions.

verses.text: and those who were listed by genealogy of the priests by their fathers’ houses, and the Levites from twenty years old and upward, in their offices by their divisions;
verses.explainer: The registration of priests is by their ancestral houses, and the Levites twenty years old and above by their responsibilities and divisions.

verses.text: and those who were listed by genealogy of all their little ones, their wives, their sons, and their daughters, through all the congregation; for in their office of trust they sanctified themselves in holiness.
verses.explainer: The registration includes all their little ones, wives, sons, and daughters -- the whole assembly, for they faithfully consecrate themselves in holiness.

thenVsNow.then: Supporting clergy families, not just individual ministers, reflects the understanding that those who serve God's community need full household provision to sustain their calling.
thenVsNow.now: Supporting clergy families, not just individual ministers, reflects the understanding that those who serve God's community need full household provision to sustain their calling.

verses.text: Also for the sons of Aaron the priests, who were in the fields of the suburbs of their cities, in every city, there were men who were mentioned by name, to give portions to all the males among the priests, and to all who were listed by genealogy among the Levites.
verses.explainer: For the priests, the sons of Aaron, in the pasturelands of their cities, men are designated by name to distribute portions to every male among the priests and to everyone registered among the Levites.

verses.text: Hezekiah did so throughout all Judah; and he did that which was good, right, and faithful before Yahweh his God.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah does this throughout all Judah. He does what is good, right, and faithful before the LORD his God.

verses.text: In every work that he began in the service of God’s house, in the law, and in the commandments, to seek his God, he did it with all his heart, and prospered.
verses.explainer: In every work he undertakes for the service of the house of God, the law, and the commandments, seeking his God, he does it with all his heart and prospers.

crossReferences.connection: Amaziah did right 'but not wholeheartedly' -- Hezekiah does everything 'with all his heart.'

wordStudies.translatedAs: Contribution, offering -- literally 'that which is
wordStudies.meaning: Contribution, offering -- literally 'that which is lifted up' or set apart. These were the portions dedicated to God and given to the priests and Levites for their support.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Faithfulness, reliability -- used to describe how
wordStudies.meaning: Faithfulness, reliability -- used to describe how the administrators managed the contributions. Financial stewardship required the same faithfulness as worship leadership.

explainer.summary: After Hezekiah's reforms, Sennacherib king of Assyria invades Judah. Hezekiah prepares physically (blocking water sources, repairing walls, making weapons) and spiritually (encouraging the people to trust God). Sennacherib sends messengers taunting Judah's God as no different from the gods of conquered nations. Hezekiah and Isaiah pray, and the LORD sends an angel who destroys the Assyrian army. Sennacherib returns home in disgrace and is murdered by his own sons. The chapter also covers Hezekiah's illness, his pride, and his wealth.
explainer.contextInBook: Sennacherib's invasion tests whether Hezekiah's faith is genuine or merely ceremonial. His response -- combining practical preparation with spiritual trust -- is the model the Chronicler presents for facing impossible situations.

verses.text: After these things and this faithfulness, Sennacherib king of Assyria came, entered into Judah, and encamped against the fortified cities, and intended to win them for himself.
verses.explainer: After these acts of faithfulness, Sennacherib king of Assyria comes and invades Judah, besieging the fortified cities and intending to conquer them.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel account of Sennacherib's invasion with additional historical details.

verses.text: When Hezekiah saw that Sennacherib had come, and that he was planning to fight against Jerusalem,
verses.explainer: When Hezekiah sees that Sennacherib has come intending to fight against Jerusalem, he acts decisively.

verses.text: he took counsel with his princes and his mighty men to stop the waters of the springs which were outside of the city, and they helped him.
verses.explainer: He plans with his officers and warriors to stop the water sources outside the city. They help him carry out the plan.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Hezekiah's water tunnel, carved through 1,750 feet of solid rock, is one of the greatest engineering achievements of the ancient world. It still exists today and can be walked through in Jerusalem.

verses.text: So, many people gathered together and they stopped all the springs and the brook that flowed through the middle of the land, saying, “Why should the kings of Assyria come, and find abundant water?”
verses.explainer: A large force assembles and stops all the springs and the stream flowing through the land, asking why the kings of Assyria should come and find plenty of water.

verses.text: He took courage, built up all the wall that was broken down, and raised it up to the towers, with the other wall outside, and strengthened Millo in David’s city, and made weapons and shields in abundance.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah strengthens himself, rebuilds the broken wall, raises towers, builds another wall outside, and strengthens the Millo in the City of David. He also makes large quantities of weapons and shields.

thenVsNow.then: Hezekiah's combination of faith and practical action -- praying AND building walls, trusting God AND making weapons -- models healthy faith that doesn't treat spiritual trust and practical effort as contradictions.
thenVsNow.now: Hezekiah's combination of faith and practical action -- praying AND building walls, trusting God AND making weapons -- models healthy faith that doesn't treat spiritual trust and practical effort as contradictions.

verses.text: He set captains of war over the people, and gathered them together to him in the wide place at the gate of the city, and spoke encouragingly to them, saying,
verses.explainer: He appoints military commanders over the people and gathers them in the square at the city gate to encourage them.

verses.text: “Be strong and courageous. Don’t be afraid or dismayed because of the king of Assyria, nor for all the multitude who is with him; for there is a greater one with us than with him.
verses.explainer: 'Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid or dismayed before the king of Assyria and all the horde with him, for there are more with us than with him.'

crossReferences.connection: Elisha's identical assurance to his servant: 'Those who are with us are more than those who are with them.'

verses.text: An arm of flesh is with him, but Yahweh our God is with us to help us and to fight our battles.” The people rested themselves on the words of Hezekiah king of Judah.
verses.explainer: 'With him is an arm of flesh, but with us is the LORD our God, to help us and to fight our battles.' And the people are strengthened by the words of Hezekiah king of Judah.

thenVsNow.then: 'An arm of flesh' versus God's help remains the fundamental choice in every crisis: will we rely on human resources alone, or trust that God's presence changes the equation?
thenVsNow.now: 'An arm of flesh' versus God's help remains the fundamental choice in every crisis: will we rely on human resources alone, or trust that God's presence changes the equation?

verses.text: After this, Sennacherib king of Assyria sent his servants to Jerusalem, (now he was before Lachish, and all his power with him), to Hezekiah king of Judah, and to all Judah who were at Jerusalem, saying,
verses.explainer: Sennacherib, while besieging Lachish with all his forces, sends his servants to Jerusalem with a message for Hezekiah and all the people of Judah.

verses.text: Thus says Sennacherib king of Assyria, “In whom do you trust, that you remain under siege in Jerusalem?
verses.explainer: Sennacherib says: 'On what are you trusting, that you remain under siege in Jerusalem?'

verses.text: Doesn’t Hezekiah persuade you, to give you over to die by famine and by thirst, saying, ‘Yahweh our God will deliver us out of the hand of the king of Assyria?’
verses.explainer: 'Hezekiah is misleading you, handing you over to die by famine and thirst, saying the LORD your God will deliver you from the king of Assyria.'

verses.text: Hasn’t the same Hezekiah taken away his high places and his altars, and commanded Judah and Jerusalem, saying, ‘You shall worship before one altar, and you shall burn incense on it?’
verses.explainer: 'Has not this same Hezekiah removed the LORD's high places and altars, telling Judah and Jerusalem to worship before one altar and burn incense on it?'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: To a polytheist like Sennacherib, reducing the number of altars looked like reducing worship. He couldn't understand that centralizing worship at one altar was strengthening it, not weakening it.

verses.text: Don’t you know what I and my fathers have done to all the peoples of the lands? Were the gods of the nations of the lands in any way able to deliver their land out of my hand?
verses.explainer: 'Do you not know what I and my fathers have done to all the peoples of other lands? Were the gods of those nations ever able to deliver their land from my hand?'

verses.text: Who was there among all the gods of those nations which my fathers utterly destroyed, that could deliver his people out of my hand, that your God should be able to deliver you out of my hand?
verses.explainer: 'Which of all the gods of those nations that my fathers destroyed could deliver his people from my hand, that your God should be able to deliver you from my hand?'

verses.text: Now therefore don’t let Hezekiah deceive you, nor persuade you in this way. Don’t believe him, for no god of any nation or kingdom was able to deliver his people out of my hand, and out of the hand of my fathers. How much less will your God deliver you out of my hand?”
verses.explainer: 'Do not let Hezekiah deceive or mislead you. Do not believe him, for no god of any nation or kingdom has been able to deliver his people from my hand or my fathers' hand. How much less will your God deliver you!'

verses.text: His servants spoke yet more against Yahweh God, and against his servant Hezekiah.
verses.explainer: His servants speak even more against the LORD God and His servant Hezekiah.

verses.text: He also wrote letters insulting Yahweh, the God of Israel, and speaking against him, saying, “As the gods of the nations of the lands, which have not delivered their people out of my hand, so shall the God of Hezekiah not deliver his people out of my hand.”
verses.explainer: He writes letters insulting the LORD God of Israel, saying: 'As the gods of the nations of the lands have not delivered their people from my hand, so the God of Hezekiah will not deliver His people.'

thenVsNow.then: Sennacherib's propaganda technique -- using past victories to create a narrative of invincibility -- is still used by powerful entities today. But past success against lesser powers doesn't predict outcomes against God.
thenVsNow.now: Sennacherib's propaganda technique -- using past victories to create a narrative of invincibility -- is still used by powerful entities today. But past success against lesser powers doesn't predict outcomes against God.

verses.text: They called out with a loud voice in the Jews’ language to the people of Jerusalem who were on the wall, to frighten them, and to trouble them; that they might take the city.
verses.explainer: They shout loudly in the Judahite language to the people of Jerusalem on the wall, trying to frighten and terrify them so they can capture the city.

crossReferences.connection: The Kings account has the fuller dialogue where Judah's officials ask the Assyrians to speak Aramaic, and they refuse.

verses.text: They spoke of the God of Jerusalem as of the gods of the peoples of the earth, which are the work of men’s hands.
verses.explainer: They speak of the God of Jerusalem as they speak of the gods of the peoples of the earth, which are the work of men's hands.

verses.text: Hezekiah the king and Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz, prayed because of this, and cried to heaven.
verses.explainer: King Hezekiah and Isaiah the prophet, son of Amoz, pray about this and cry out to heaven.

annotations.word: Hezekiah the king and Isaiah the prophet

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah's account of Hezekiah's prayer, spreading Sennacherib's letter before the LORD in the temple.

verses.text: Yahweh sent an angel, who cut off all the mighty men of valor, and the leaders and captains, in the camp of the king of Assyria. So he returned with shame of face to his own land. When he had come into the house of his god, those who came out of his own bowels killed him there with the sword.
verses.explainer: The LORD sends an angel who destroys every mighty warrior, commander, and officer in the camp of the king of Assyria. He returns home in disgrace, and when he enters the house of his god, some of his own sons strike him down with the sword.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Sennacherib's own records confirm he besieged Jerusalem but never claim to have conquered it -- a notable omission from a king who boasted of all his victories. He describes Hezekiah as 'shut up like a caged bird' but never says he took the cage.

thenVsNow.then: Sennacherib returned to his own temple and was killed by his own sons -- the ultimate irony for a king who mocked other gods. The 'arm of flesh' failed at every level: militarily, politically, and personally.
thenVsNow.now: Sennacherib returned to his own temple and was killed by his own sons -- the ultimate irony for a king who mocked other gods. The 'arm of flesh' failed at every level: militarily, politically, and personally.

verses.text: Thus Yahweh saved Hezekiah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem from the hand of Sennacherib the king of Assyria and from the hand of all others, and guided them on every side.
verses.explainer: So the LORD saves Hezekiah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem from Sennacherib and from all others, and gives them rest on every side.

verses.text: Many brought gifts to Yahweh to Jerusalem, and precious things to Hezekiah king of Judah; so that he was exalted in the sight of all nations from then on.
verses.explainer: Many bring gifts to the LORD at Jerusalem and precious things to Hezekiah king of Judah, so that he is exalted in the sight of all nations from then on.

verses.text: In those days Hezekiah was terminally ill, and he prayed to Yahweh; and he spoke to him, and gave him a sign.
verses.explainer: In those days Hezekiah becomes mortally ill. He prays to the LORD, who answers him and gives him a sign.

crossReferences.connection: The Kings account provides full details of Hezekiah's illness, Isaiah's prophecy, Hezekiah's prayer, and the shadow moving backward on the sundial.

verses.text: But Hezekiah didn’t reciprocate appropriate to the benefit done for him, because his heart was lifted up. Therefore there was wrath on him, and on Judah and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: But Hezekiah does not respond to the kindness shown him, because his heart is proud. So wrath comes on him and on Judah and Jerusalem.

thenVsNow.then: Hezekiah's pride after receiving extraordinary blessings warns that spiritual leaders are most vulnerable to pride immediately after their greatest successes.
thenVsNow.now: Hezekiah's pride after receiving extraordinary blessings warns that spiritual leaders are most vulnerable to pride immediately after their greatest successes.

verses.text: Notwithstanding Hezekiah humbled himself for the pride of his heart, both he and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that Yahweh’s wrath didn’t come on them in the days of Hezekiah.
verses.explainer: Then Hezekiah humbles himself for the pride of his heart, both he and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that the wrath of the LORD does not come on them in Hezekiah's days.

verses.text: Hezekiah had exceedingly much riches and honor. He provided himself with treasuries for silver, for gold, for precious stones, for spices, for shields, and for all kinds of valuable vessels;
verses.explainer: Hezekiah has very great riches and honor. He makes treasuries for silver, gold, precious stones, spices, shields, and all kinds of valuable articles.

verses.text: also storehouses for the increase of grain, new wine, and oil; and stalls for all kinds of animals, and flocks in folds.
verses.explainer: He also has storehouses for the harvest of grain, wine, and oil, and stalls for all kinds of livestock, and sheepfolds.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The catalog of Hezekiah's wealth parallels Solomon's, reinforcing the Chronicler's portrait of Hezekiah as a second Solomon -- a king whose faithfulness produced Solomonic prosperity.

verses.text: Moreover he provided for himself cities, and possessions of flocks and herds in abundance; for God had given him abundant posessions.
verses.explainer: He makes cities for himself and acquires large flocks and herds, for God gives him very great possessions.

verses.text: This same Hezekiah also stopped the upper spring of the waters of Gihon, and brought them straight down on the west side of David’s city. Hezekiah prospered in all his works.
verses.explainer: This same Hezekiah stops the upper outlet of the waters of Gihon and directs them down to the west side of the City of David. Hezekiah prospers in all his works.

verses.text: However concerning the ambassadors of the princes of Babylon, who sent to him to inquire of the wonder that was done in the land, God left him, to try him, that he might know all that was in his heart.
verses.explainer: However, in the matter of the envoys of the princes of Babylon who sent to him to inquire about the sign done in the land, God leaves him to test him, that He may know all that is in his heart.

crossReferences.connection: The Kings account tells how Hezekiah showed the Babylonian envoys all his treasures, and Isaiah rebuked him for it.

thenVsNow.then: God's 'testing by withdrawal' -- stepping back to reveal what's genuinely in someone's heart -- is a pattern many believers experience. Times when God feels absent often reveal our true spiritual condition.
thenVsNow.now: God's 'testing by withdrawal' -- stepping back to reveal what's genuinely in someone's heart -- is a pattern many believers experience. Times when God feels absent often reveal our true spiritual condition.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Hezekiah, and his good deeds, behold, they are written in the vision of Isaiah the prophet the son of Amoz, in the book of the kings of Judah and Israel.
verses.explainer: The rest of Hezekiah's acts and his good deeds are written in the vision of Isaiah the prophet son of Amoz, and in the Book of the Kings of Judah and Israel.

verses.text: Hezekiah slept with his fathers, and they buried him in the ascent of the tombs of the sons of David. All Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem honored him at his death. Manasseh his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Hezekiah rests with his fathers and is buried in the ascent of the tombs of the sons of David. All Judah and Jerusalem honor him at his death. His son Manasseh succeeds him.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Flesh -- when Hezekiah calls Assyria's power 'an a
wordStudies.meaning: Flesh -- when Hezekiah calls Assyria's power 'an arm of flesh,' this word emphasizes human mortality and limitation in contrast to God's eternal power.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Angel, messenger -- the 'angel of the LORD' who de
wordStudies.meaning: Angel, messenger -- the 'angel of the LORD' who destroys the Assyrian army is God's agent of judgment, representing divine power acting in human history.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To test, try, prove -- God 'tested' Hezekiah by wi
wordStudies.meaning: To test, try, prove -- God 'tested' Hezekiah by withdrawing His perceptible presence, revealing the king's heart. Biblical testing aims at revelation and growth, not entrapment.

explainer.summary: Manasseh reigns fifty-five years and commits the worst idolatry in Judah's history: rebuilding high places, erecting Baal altars, making Asherah poles, worshiping the stars, placing an idol in the temple, practicing sorcery, and sacrificing his sons in fire. God sends prophets whom Manasseh ignores. Then the Assyrians capture him with hooks and chains and take him to Babylon. In distress, Manasseh humbles himself, prays, and God restores him to Jerusalem. He removes the idols and restores the LORD's altar. His son Amon reigns briefly but is assassinated for his wickedness.
themes: The uniqueness of Manasseh's conversion in Chronicles
explainer.contextInBook: Manasseh's story is perhaps the most theologically important in 2 Chronicles. His repentance, found only here and not in 2 Kings, proves the Chronicler's central message: no matter how far someone has fallen, genuine humility and seeking God will be met with restoration.

verses.text: Manasseh was twelve years old when he began to reign, and he reigned fifty-five years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Manasseh is twelve years old when he becomes king and reigns fifty-five years in Jerusalem -- the longest reign in Judah's history.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel account of Manasseh's reign, which does not include his repentance.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, after the abominations of the nations whom Yahweh cast out before the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: He does evil in the LORD's sight, following the abominations of the nations the LORD had driven out before Israel.

verses.text: For he built again the high places which Hezekiah his father had broken down; and he raised up altars for the Baals, made Asheroth, and worshiped all the army of the sky, and served them.
verses.explainer: He rebuilds the high places his father Hezekiah had torn down, erects altars to the Baals, makes Asherah poles, and worships and serves the host of heaven.

thenVsNow.then: Manasseh's destruction of his father's reforms shows that institutional progress is never permanent -- every generation must make its own commitment to faithfulness. One generation's revival can be the next generation's starting point for regression.
thenVsNow.now: Manasseh's destruction of his father's reforms shows that institutional progress is never permanent -- every generation must make its own commitment to faithfulness. One generation's revival can be the next generation's starting point for regression.

verses.text: He built altars in Yahweh’s house, of which Yahweh said, “My name shall be in Jerusalem forever.”
verses.explainer: He builds altars in the house of the LORD, about which the LORD had said, 'In Jerusalem shall my name be forever.'

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that His name would be in the temple forever -- which Manasseh desecrated by placing pagan altars there.

verses.text: He built altars for all the army of the sky in the two courts of Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: He builds altars for all the host of heaven in the two courts of the house of the LORD.

verses.text: He also made his children to pass through the fire in the valley of the son of Hinnom. He practiced sorcery, divination, and witchcraft, and dealt with those who had familiar spirits, and with wizards. He did much evil in Yahweh’s sight, to provoke him to anger.
verses.explainer: He burns his sons as offerings in the Valley of Ben-hinnom, practices soothsaying, uses divination, practices sorcery, and deals with mediums and necromancers. He does much evil in the LORD's sight, provoking Him to anger.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The catalog of Manasseh's sins matches almost exactly the list of forbidden practices in Deuteronomy 18:10-12. The Chronicler shows that Manasseh violated every prohibition specifically -- this was not accidental drift but systematic apostasy.

crossReferences.connection: The Mosaic prohibitions against every practice Manasseh adopted.

verses.text: He set the engraved image of the idol, which he had made, in God’s house, of which God said to David and to Solomon his son, “In this house, and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, will I put my name forever.
verses.explainer: He sets the carved image of the idol he made in the house of God, about which God told David and Solomon, 'In this house and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, I will put my name forever.'

verses.text: I will not any more remove the foot of Israel from off the land which I have appointed for your fathers, if only they will observe to do all that I have commanded them, even all the law, the statutes, and the ordinances given by Moses.”
verses.explainer: 'I will no more remove the foot of Israel from the land I appointed for your fathers, if only they are careful to do all I commanded them, all the law, statutes, and rules given through Moses.'

verses.text: Manasseh seduced Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, so that they did more evil than did the nations whom Yahweh destroyed before the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: Manasseh leads Judah and Jerusalem astray to do more evil than the nations the LORD had destroyed before the people of Israel.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to Manasseh, and to his people; but they didn’t listen.
verses.explainer: The LORD speaks to Manasseh and his people, but they pay no attention.

verses.text: Therefore Yahweh brought on them the captains of the army of the king of Assyria, who took Manasseh in chains, bound him with fetters, and carried him to Babylon.
verses.explainer: The LORD brings against them the commanders of the army of the king of Assyria, who capture Manasseh with hooks, bind him with bronze chains, and take him to Babylon.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Taking Manasseh to Babylon rather than Nineveh (Assyria's capital) reflects the historical reality that Assyria maintained a secondary palace in Babylon. This detail suggests the account is historically grounded, not invented.

verses.text: When he was in distress, he begged Yahweh his God, and humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers.
verses.explainer: When he is in distress, he entreats the favor of the LORD his God and humbles himself greatly before the God of his fathers.

thenVsNow.then: Manasseh's conversion in captivity shows that sometimes the worst circumstances produce the most genuine transformation. What looked like destruction was actually the context for repentance.
thenVsNow.now: Manasseh's conversion in captivity shows that sometimes the worst circumstances produce the most genuine transformation. What looked like destruction was actually the context for repentance.

verses.text: He prayed to him; and he was entreated by him, and heard his supplication, and brought him again to Jerusalem into his kingdom. Then Manasseh knew that Yahweh was God.
verses.explainer: He prays to God, and God is moved by his entreaty, hears his plea, and brings him back to Jerusalem into his kingdom. Then Manasseh knows that the LORD is God.

crossReferences.connection: The direct fulfillment of God's promise: 'If my people humble themselves and pray and seek my face... I will hear from heaven.'

thenVsNow.then: That God would restore even Manasseh -- the worst king who committed the worst sins -- after genuine repentance is perhaps the strongest statement of divine mercy in the Old Testament.
thenVsNow.now: That God would restore even Manasseh -- the worst king who committed the worst sins -- after genuine repentance is perhaps the strongest statement of divine mercy in the Old Testament.

verses.text: Now after this, he built an outer wall to David’s city, on the west side of Gihon, in the valley, even to the entrance at the fish gate. He encircled Ophel with it, and raised it up to a very great height; and he put valiant captains in all the fortified cities of Judah.
verses.explainer: Afterward he builds an outer wall for the City of David west of Gihon in the valley, extending to the entrance of the Fish Gate and encircling the Ophel. He stations military commanders in all the fortified cities of Judah.

verses.text: He took away the foreign gods, and the idol out of Yahweh’s house, and all the altars that he had built in the mountain of Yahweh’s house, and in Jerusalem, and cast them out of the city.
verses.explainer: He removes the foreign gods and the idol from the house of the LORD, and all the altars he had built on the mountain of the house of the LORD and in Jerusalem, casting them outside the city.

thenVsNow.then: True repentance requires not just internal change but active reversal of damage caused -- Manasseh didn't just feel sorry; he removed the idols he had installed.
thenVsNow.now: True repentance requires not just internal change but active reversal of damage caused -- Manasseh didn't just feel sorry; he removed the idols he had installed.

verses.text: He built up Yahweh’s altar, and offered sacrifices of peace offerings and of thanksgiving on it, and commanded Judah to serve Yahweh, the God of Israel.
verses.explainer: He restores the altar of the LORD and sacrifices peace offerings and thank offerings on it, and commands Judah to serve the LORD God of Israel.

verses.text: Nevertheless the people sacrificed still in the high places, but only to Yahweh their God.
verses.explainer: The people still sacrifice at the high places, but only to the LORD their God -- incomplete reform reflecting the difficulty of reversing deeply rooted practices.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Manasseh's inability to fully eliminate high place worship shows that some changes take generations. Even a repentant king with absolute power could not instantly reverse decades of ingrained practice.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Manasseh, and his prayer to his God, and the words of the seers who spoke to him in the name of Yahweh, the God of Israel, behold, they are written among the acts of the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: The rest of Manasseh's acts, his prayer to his God, and the words of the seers who spoke to him in the name of the LORD God of Israel are recorded in the annals of the kings of Israel.

verses.text: His prayer also, and how God was entreated of him, and all his sin and his trespass, and the places in which he built high places, and set up the Asherah poles and the engraved images, before he humbled himself: behold, they are written in the history of Hozai.
verses.explainer: His prayer, how God was moved by his entreaty, all his sin and unfaithfulness, the sites where he built high places and set up Asherah poles and images before he humbled himself -- all are recorded in the records of the seers.

verses.text: So Manasseh slept with his fathers, and they buried him in his own house; and Amon his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: Manasseh rests with his fathers and is buried in his own house. His son Amon succeeds him.

verses.text: Amon was twenty-two years old when he began to reign; and he reigned two years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Amon is twenty-two when he becomes king and reigns two years in Jerusalem.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight, as did Manasseh his father; and Amon sacrificed to all the engraved images which Manasseh his father had made, and served them.
verses.explainer: He does evil in the LORD's sight as his father Manasseh had done. Amon sacrifices to and serves all the carved images Manasseh had made.

verses.text: He didn’t humble himself before Yahweh, as Manasseh his father had humbled himself; but this same Amon trespassed more and more.
verses.explainer: He does not humble himself before the LORD as his father Manasseh humbled himself. Instead, Amon increases his guilt.

thenVsNow.then: Amon had the most dramatic repentance story in Judah's history -- his own father's -- and still chose wickedness. Having examples of redemption doesn't guarantee we'll follow them.
thenVsNow.now: Amon had the most dramatic repentance story in Judah's history -- his own father's -- and still chose wickedness. Having examples of redemption doesn't guarantee we'll follow them.

verses.text: His servants conspired against him, and put him to death in his own house.
verses.explainer: His servants conspire against him and assassinate him in his own house.

verses.text: But the people of the land killed all those who had conspired against king Amon; and the people of the land made Josiah his son king in his place.
verses.explainer: The people of the land strike down all who conspired against King Amon, and they make his son Josiah king in his place.

crossReferences.connection: Josiah's accession at age eight begins the last great reform in Judah's history.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To humble oneself, be subdued -- the crucial verb
wordStudies.meaning: To humble oneself, be subdued -- the crucial verb from 2 Chronicles 7:14 that Manasseh fulfills and Amon refuses. This word implies bending the will, lowering oneself before God's authority.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To be entreated, to be moved by prayer -- when God
wordStudies.meaning: To be entreated, to be moved by prayer -- when God 'was moved' by Manasseh's prayer, this word indicates God's willingness to respond to genuine supplication. God is not immovable; prayer genuinely affects divine action.

explainer.summary: Josiah begins seeking God at age sixteen and purging idolatry at age twenty, years before finding the Book of the Law. He destroys altars, Asherah poles, and carved images throughout Judah and into northern territory. At age twenty-six, he orders temple repairs. During the renovation, Hilkiah the priest discovers the Book of the Law. When it is read to Josiah, he tears his robes in anguish, realizing how far the nation has strayed. The prophetess Huldah confirms that judgment is coming but will be delayed because of Josiah's tender heart. Josiah then leads a covenant renewal ceremony.
explainer.contextInBook: Josiah is the last reformer king, and his story demonstrates both the power and the limits of reform. His personal faithfulness could delay but not cancel the accumulated judgment from Manasseh's era -- timing and institutional damage matter.

verses.text: Josiah was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned thirty-one years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Josiah is eight years old when he becomes king, and he reigns thirty-one years in Jerusalem.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel account of Josiah's reign, with the same basic chronology.

verses.text: He did that which was right in Yahweh’s eyes, and walked in the ways of David his father, and didn’t turn away to the right hand or to the left.
verses.explainer: He does right in the LORD's eyes, walking in the ways of his father David and not turning aside to the right or the left.

verses.text: For in the eighth year of his reign, while he was yet young, he began to seek after the God of David his father; and in the twelfth year he began to purge Judah and Jerusalem from the high places, the Asherah poles, the engraved images, and the molten images.
verses.explainer: In the eighth year of his reign, while still young, he begins to seek the God of his father David. In the twelfth year he begins to purge Judah and Jerusalem of high places, Asherah poles, carved images, and metal images.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Chronicler's chronology differs from Kings by noting Josiah's spiritual journey in stages: seeking God at 16, purging idolatry at 20, repairing the temple at 26, finding the Law at 26. This shows reform as a progressive process, not a single event.

thenVsNow.then: Josiah's seeking God before knowing the full Scripture shows that genuine faith can precede complete knowledge -- people can start following God with incomplete understanding and grow into fuller truth.
thenVsNow.now: Josiah's seeking God before knowing the full Scripture shows that genuine faith can precede complete knowledge -- people can start following God with incomplete understanding and grow into fuller truth.

verses.text: They broke down the altars of the Baals in his presence; and he cut down the incense altars that were on high above them. He broke the Asherah poles, the engraved images, and the molten images in pieces, made dust of them, and scattered it on the graves of those who had sacrificed to them.
verses.explainer: They tear down the altars of the Baals in his presence. He cuts down the incense altars above them, smashes the Asherah poles, carved images, and metal images, grinds them to dust, and scatters it on the graves of those who had sacrificed to them.

verses.text: He burned the bones of the priests on their altars, and purged Judah and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: He burns the bones of the priests on their altars, purging Judah and Jerusalem.

crossReferences.connection: The anonymous prophet who predicted centuries earlier that a king named Josiah would burn priests' bones on Jeroboam's altar -- now fulfilled.

verses.text: He did this in the cities of Manasseh, Ephraim, and Simeon, even to Naphtali, around in their ruins.
verses.explainer: In the cities of Manasseh, Ephraim, Simeon, and as far as Naphtali, in their ruins all around,

verses.text: He broke down the altars, and beat the Asherah poles and the engraved images into powder, and cut down all the incense altars throughout all the land of Israel, then returned to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: He tears down the altars, beats the Asherah poles and images to powder, and cuts down all the incense altars throughout the land of Israel. Then he returns to Jerusalem.

annotations.word: throughout all the land of Israel

verses.text: Now in the eighteenth year of his reign, when he had purged the land and the house, he sent Shaphan the son of Azaliah, and Maaseiah the governor of the city, and Joah the son of Joahaz the recorder, to repair Yahweh his God’s house.
verses.explainer: In the eighteenth year of his reign, when he has purged the land and the temple, he sends Shaphan, Maaseiah the governor of the city, and Joah the recorder to repair the house of the LORD his God.

verses.text: They came to Hilkiah the high priest, and delivered the money that was brought into God’s house, which the Levites, the keepers of the threshold, had gathered from the hands of Manasseh, Ephraim, of all the remnant of Israel, of all Judah and Benjamin, and of the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: They go to Hilkiah the high priest and deliver the money brought into the house of God, which the Levites who guard the threshold have collected from Manasseh, Ephraim, the remnant of Israel, all Judah, Benjamin, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Contributions came from both northern and southern populations, showing that Josiah's reform had some support beyond Judah's borders -- continuing Hezekiah's all-Israel vision.

verses.text: They delivered it into the hands of the workmen who had the oversight of Yahweh’s house; and the workmen who labored in Yahweh’s house gave it to mend and repair the house.
verses.explainer: They give it to the workmen who have oversight of the house of the LORD, who pay the workers who repair and restore the temple.

verses.text: They gave it to the carpenters and to the builders, to buy cut stone and timber for couplings, and to make beams for the houses which the kings of Judah had destroyed.
verses.explainer: They give it to the carpenters and builders to buy quarried stone and timber for beams and for the buildings that the kings of Judah had let fall into ruin.

verses.text: The men did the work faithfully. Their overseers were Jahath and Obadiah, the Levites, of the sons of Merari; and Zechariah and Meshullam, of the sons of the Kohathites, to give direction; and others of the Levites, who were all skillful with musical instruments.
verses.explainer: The men work faithfully. Their overseers are Jahath and Obadiah, Levites of the sons of Merari, and Zechariah and Meshullam of the sons of the Kohathites, who direct the work. The Levites are all skilled with musical instruments.

verses.text: Also they were over the bearers of burdens, and directed all who did the work in every kind of service. Of the Levites, there were scribes, officials, and gatekeepers.
verses.explainer: They are over the burden-bearers and direct all who do work in every kind of service. Some Levites serve as scribes, officials, and gatekeepers.

verses.text: When they brought out the money that was brought into Yahweh’s house, Hilkiah the priest found the book of Yahweh’s law given by Moses.
verses.explainer: While bringing out the money that had been brought into the house of the LORD, Hilkiah the priest finds the Book of the Law of the LORD given through Moses.

thenVsNow.then: That an entire nation could lose its own founding document -- the Book of God's Law -- in its own temple warns how quickly sacred texts can be forgotten when institutional faithfulness fails.
thenVsNow.now: That an entire nation could lose its own founding document -- the Book of God's Law -- in its own temple warns how quickly sacred texts can be forgotten when institutional faithfulness fails.

verses.text: Hilkiah answered Shaphan the scribe, “I have found the book of the law in Yahweh’s house.” So Hilkiah delivered the book to Shaphan.
verses.explainer: Hilkiah tells Shaphan the secretary: 'I have found the Book of the Law in the house of the LORD.' Hilkiah gives the book to Shaphan.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'finding' of the Law book was likely not accidental but the result of Josiah's ordered repairs uncovering what had been buried or hidden during Manasseh's desecrations. Reform created the conditions for rediscovery.

verses.text: Shaphan carried the book to the king, and moreover brought back word to the king, saying, “All that was committed to your servants, they are doing.
verses.explainer: Shaphan brings the book to the king and reports: 'All that was committed to your servants they are doing.'

verses.text: They have emptied out the money that was found in Yahweh’s house, and have delivered it into the hand of the overseers, and into the hand of the workmen.”
verses.explainer: They have emptied the money found in the house of the LORD and delivered it to the overseers and workers.

verses.text: Shaphan the scribe told the king, saying, “Hilkiah the priest has delivered me a book.” Shaphan read from it to the king.
verses.explainer: Then Shaphan the secretary tells the king that Hilkiah the priest has given him a book. And Shaphan reads from it before the king.

verses.text: When the king had heard the words of the law, he tore his clothes.
verses.explainer: When the king hears the words of the Law, he tears his clothes.

thenVsNow.then: Josiah's immediate, visceral response to Scripture -- tearing his clothes in anguish -- models what happens when God's Word truly penetrates: it produces deep awareness of the gap between God's standard and human reality.
thenVsNow.now: Josiah's immediate, visceral response to Scripture -- tearing his clothes in anguish -- models what happens when God's Word truly penetrates: it produces deep awareness of the gap between God's standard and human reality.

verses.text: The king commanded Hilkiah, Ahikam the son of Shaphan, Abdon the son of Micah, Shaphan the scribe, and Asaiah the king’s servant, saying,
verses.explainer: The king commands Hilkiah, Ahikam son of Shaphan, Abdon son of Micah, Shaphan the secretary, and Asaiah the king's servant:

verses.text: “Go inquire of Yahweh for me, and for those who are left in Israel and in Judah, concerning the words of the book that is found; for great is Yahweh’s wrath that is poured out on us, because our fathers have not kept Yahweh’s word, to do according to all that is written in this book.”
verses.explainer: 'Go, inquire of the LORD for me and for those who are left in Israel and in Judah, concerning the words of the book that has been found. For great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out on us, because our fathers did not keep the word of the LORD or do according to all that is written in this book.'

verses.text: So Hilkiah, and they whom the king had commanded, went to Huldah the prophetess, the wife of Shallum the son of Tokhath, the son of Hasrah, keeper of the wardrobe (now she lived in Jerusalem in the second quarter), and they spoke to her to that effect.
verses.explainer: Hilkiah and those the king sends go to Huldah the prophetess, wife of Shallum son of Tokhath son of Hasrah, keeper of the wardrobe. She lives in Jerusalem in the Second Quarter. They speak to her about the matter.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Huldah's role demonstrates that prophetic authority in ancient Israel was not exclusively male. The delegation included the high priest and senior officials, yet they deferred to her as God's spokesperson.

verses.text: She said to them, “Yahweh, the God of Israel says: ‘Tell the man who sent you to me,
verses.explainer: She tells them: 'Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: Tell the man who sent you to me,

verses.text: “Yahweh says, ‘Behold, I will bring evil on this place, and on its inhabitants, even all the curses that are written in the book which they have read before the king of Judah.
verses.explainer: 'Thus says the LORD: Behold, I will bring disaster upon this place and upon its inhabitants, all the curses that are written in the book that was read before the king of Judah.'

crossReferences.connection: The covenant curses Moses pronounced for disobedience, which Huldah confirms will now be executed.

verses.text: Because they have forsaken me, and have burned incense to other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands; therefore my wrath is poured out on this place, and it will not be quenched.’”’
verses.explainer: 'Because they have forsaken me and have made offerings to other gods, that they might provoke me to anger with all the works of their hands, my wrath will be poured out on this place and will not be quenched.'

verses.text: But to the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of Yahweh, you shall tell him this, ‘Yahweh, the God of Israel says: “About the words which you have heard,
verses.explainer: 'But to the king of Judah, who sent you to inquire of the LORD, thus shall you say to him:

verses.text: because your heart was tender, and you humbled yourself before God, when you heard his words against this place, and against its inhabitants, and have humbled yourself before me, and have torn your clothes, and wept before me, I also have heard you,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: 'Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: Regarding the words that you have heard, because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before God when you heard His words against this place and its inhabitants, and you humbled yourself before me and tore your clothes and wept before me, I also have heard you, declares the LORD.'

thenVsNow.then: A 'tender heart' before Scripture -- one that is genuinely moved and changed by God's Word rather than merely informed by it -- is what distinguishes transformative faith from mere religious knowledge.
thenVsNow.now: A 'tender heart' before Scripture -- one that is genuinely moved and changed by God's Word rather than merely informed by it -- is what distinguishes transformative faith from mere religious knowledge.

verses.text:
“Behold, I will gather you to your fathers, and you will be gathered to your grave in peace. Your eyes won’t see all the evil that I will bring on this place and on its inhabitants.”’”
They brought back word to the king.
verses.explainer: 'I will gather you to your fathers, and you shall be gathered to your grave in peace, and your eyes shall not see all the disaster that I will bring upon this place and its inhabitants.' They bring word back to the king.

annotations.word: gathered to your grave in peace

verses.text: Then the king sent and gathered together all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Then the king sends and gathers all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem.

verses.text: The king went up to Yahweh’s house, with all the men of Judah and the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the Levites, and all the people, both great and small; and he read in their hearing all the words of the book of the covenant that was found in Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: The king goes up to the house of the LORD with all the men of Judah, the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the priests, the Levites, and all the people both great and small. He reads in their hearing all the words of the Book of the Covenant found in the house of the LORD.

annotations.word: read in their hearing all the words

crossReferences.connection: Moses commanded that the Law be read publicly every seven years. Josiah fulfills this long-neglected practice.

verses.text: The king stood in his place, and made a covenant before Yahweh, to walk after Yahweh, and to keep his commandments, and his testimonies, and his statutes, with all his heart, and with all his soul, to perform the words of the covenant that were written in this book.
verses.explainer: The king stands in his place and makes a covenant before the LORD to walk after the LORD, to keep His commandments, testimonies, and statutes with all his heart and all his soul, to perform the words of the covenant written in this book.

verses.text: He caused all who were found in Jerusalem and Benjamin to stand. The inhabitants of Jerusalem did according to the covenant of God, the God of their fathers.
verses.explainer: He makes all who are present in Jerusalem and Benjamin stand to the covenant. And the inhabitants of Jerusalem act according to the covenant of God, the God of their fathers.

thenVsNow.then: Josiah's covenant renewal ceremony -- public reading of Scripture, personal commitment, communal participation -- models the elements of genuine spiritual renewal in any era.
thenVsNow.now: Josiah's covenant renewal ceremony -- public reading of Scripture, personal commitment, communal participation -- models the elements of genuine spiritual renewal in any era.

verses.text: Josiah took away all the abominations out of all the countries that belonged to the children of Israel, and made all who were found in Israel to serve, even to serve Yahweh their God. All his days they didn’t depart from following Yahweh, the God of their fathers.
verses.explainer: Josiah removes all the abominations from all the territories belonging to the people of Israel and makes all who are in Israel serve the LORD their God. All his days they do not turn from following the LORD, the God of their fathers.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Law, instruction, teaching -- the Book of the Law
wordStudies.meaning: Law, instruction, teaching -- the Book of the Law (sefer ha-torah) found in the temple was Israel's covenant constitution. Its rediscovery sparked national transformation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Tender, soft, responsive -- describing Josiah's he
wordStudies.meaning: Tender, soft, responsive -- describing Josiah's heart when confronted with God's Word. The opposite of 'hard-hearted,' this word implies vulnerability and receptiveness to divine truth.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Covenant -- a binding agreement between two partie
wordStudies.meaning: Covenant -- a binding agreement between two parties. Josiah's covenant renewal renewed the Sinai covenant relationship, committing the nation to obey the rediscovered Law.

explainer.summary: Josiah celebrates the greatest Passover since Samuel's time, even exceeding Hezekiah's. He personally contributes 30,000 lambs and goats and 3,000 bulls. The celebration is meticulously organized with Levitical musicians, gatekeepers, and priests all serving in their appointed roles. Then comes the devastating reversal: Pharaoh Neco of Egypt marches north to fight at Carchemish, and Josiah inexplicably intercepts him. Despite Neco's warning that God has sent him, Josiah disguises himself and enters battle, where he is fatally wounded by archers. His death at Megiddo begins Judah's rapid final decline.
themes: Refusing to hear God through unexpected sources
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 35 is the pivot between hope and tragedy. Josiah's Passover represents the highest point of worship in 2 Chronicles after Solomon, but his death at Megiddo signals that the accumulated judgment from Manasseh's era cannot be reversed even by the best king.

verses.text: Josiah kept a Passover to Yahweh in Jerusalem. They killed the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month.
verses.explainer: Josiah keeps a Passover to the LORD in Jerusalem. They slaughter the Passover lamb on the fourteenth day of the first month.

annotations.word: the fourteenth day of the first month

verses.text: He set the priests in their offices, and encouraged them in the service of Yahweh’s house.
verses.explainer: He sets the priests in their duties and encourages them for the service of the house of the LORD.

verses.text: He said to the Levites who taught all Israel, who were holy to Yahweh, “Put the holy ark in the house which Solomon the son of David king of Israel built. It will no longer be a burden on your shoulders. Now serve Yahweh your God, and his people Israel.
verses.explainer: He says to the Levites who teach all Israel and are holy to the LORD: 'Put the holy ark in the house that Solomon son of David, king of Israel, built. You need not carry it on your shoulders anymore. Now serve the LORD your God and His people Israel.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Josiah's command to return the ark to the temple is the last mention of the Ark of the Covenant in historical narrative. What happened to it after this -- whether destroyed by Babylon, hidden, or lost -- remains one of the great mysteries of biblical history.

verses.text: Prepare yourselves after your fathers’ houses by your divisions, according to the writing of David king of Israel, and according to the writing of Solomon his son.
verses.explainer: 'Prepare yourselves by your fathers' houses and divisions, according to the writing of David king of Israel and the document of Solomon his son.'

crossReferences.connection: David's original organization of Levitical service that Josiah restores for this Passover.

verses.text: Stand in the holy place according to the divisions of the fathers’ houses of your brothers the children of the people, and let there be for each a portion of a fathers’ house of the Levites.
verses.explainer: 'Stand in the Holy Place according to the groupings of the ancestral houses of your brothers the lay people, and let there be for each a portion of a Levitical ancestral house.'

verses.text: Kill the Passover, sanctify yourselves, and prepare for your brothers, to do according to Yahweh’s word by Moses.”
verses.explainer: 'Slaughter the Passover lamb, consecrate yourselves, and prepare for your brothers, to do according to the word of the LORD by Moses.'

verses.text: Josiah gave to the children of the people, of the flock, lambs and young goats, all of them for the Passover offerings, to all who were present, to the number of thirty thousand, and three thousand bulls. These were of the king’s substance.
verses.explainer: Josiah contributes to the lay people -- to all who are present -- 30,000 lambs and young goats for the Passover offerings, and 3,000 bulls, all from the king's possessions.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The scale of Josiah's contribution dwarfed even Hezekiah's generous provision, making this the most lavishly supported Passover in recorded history.

verses.text: His princes gave for a freewill offering to the people, to the priests, and to the Levites. Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel, the rulers of God’s house, gave to the priests for the Passover offerings two thousand six hundred small livestock, and three hundred head of cattle.
verses.explainer: His officials also contribute willingly to the people, the priests, and the Levites. Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel, the chief officers of the house of God, give 2,600 Passover offerings and 300 bulls to the priests.

verses.text: Conaniah also, and Shemaiah and Nethanel, his brothers, and Hashabiah, Jeiel, and Jozabad, the chiefs of the Levites, gave to the Levites for the Passover offerings five thousand small livestock and five hundred head of cattle.
verses.explainer: Conaniah, Shemaiah, Nethanel, Hashabiah, Jeiel, and Jozabad, the chiefs of the Levites, contribute 5,000 Passover offerings and 500 bulls to the Levites.

verses.text: So the service was prepared, and the priests stood in their place, and the Levites by their divisions, according to the king’s commandment.
verses.explainer: The service is prepared, the priests stand in their places, and the Levites in their divisions, according to the king's command.

verses.text: They killed the Passover, and the priests sprinkled the blood which they received of their hand, and the Levites flayed them.
verses.explainer: They slaughter the Passover lamb. The priests sprinkle the blood received from the Levites, and the Levites skin the animals.

verses.text: They removed the burnt offerings, that they might give them according to the divisions of the fathers’ houses of the children of the people, to offer to Yahweh, as it is written in the book of Moses. They did the same with the cattle.
verses.explainer: They set aside the burnt offerings to give them to the groupings of the lay people to offer to the LORD, as written in the Book of Moses. They do the same with the bulls.

verses.text: They roasted the Passover with fire according to the ordinance. They boiled the holy offerings in pots, in cauldrons, and in pans, and carried them quickly to all the children of the people.
verses.explainer: They roast the Passover lamb with fire according to the rule, and boil the holy offerings in pots, cauldrons, and pans, and carry them quickly to all the lay people.

crossReferences.connection: The original Passover instructions specifying that the lamb must be roasted with fire, not boiled.

verses.text: Afterward they prepared for themselves and for the priests, because the priests the sons of Aaron were busy with offering the burnt offerings and the fat until night. Therefore the Levites prepared for themselves and for the priests the sons of Aaron.
verses.explainer: Afterward they prepare for themselves and for the priests, because the priests the sons of Aaron are busy offering the burnt offerings and the fat until night. So the Levites prepare for themselves and for the Aaronic priests.

verses.text: The singers the sons of Asaph were in their place, according to the commandment of David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king’s seer; and the gatekeepers were at every gate. They didn’t need to depart from their service, because their brothers the Levites prepared for them.
verses.explainer: The singers, the sons of Asaph, are in their places according to the command of David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king's seer. The gatekeepers are at each gate; they do not need to leave their posts because their Levite brothers prepare for them.

thenVsNow.then: The seamless coordination of this Passover -- priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers all serving in their roles -- shows what institutional worship looks like when every member functions in their appointed capacity.
thenVsNow.now: The seamless coordination of this Passover -- priests, Levites, singers, gatekeepers all serving in their roles -- shows what institutional worship looks like when every member functions in their appointed capacity.

verses.text: So all the service of Yahweh was prepared the same day, to keep the Passover, and to offer burnt offerings on Yahweh’s altar, according to the commandment of king Josiah.
