verses.explainer: All the service of the LORD is prepared that day, to keep the Passover and to offer burnt offerings on the altar of the LORD, according to the command of King Josiah.

verses.text: The children of Israel who were present kept the Passover at that time, and the feast of unleavened bread seven days.
verses.explainer: The people of Israel who are present keep the Passover and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days.

verses.text: There was no Passover like that kept in Israel from the days of Samuel the prophet, nor did any of the kings of Israel keep such a Passover as Josiah kept, with the priests, the Levites, and all Judah and Israel who were present, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: No Passover like it has been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel kept such a Passover as Josiah kept, with the priests, Levites, all Judah and Israel who were present, and the inhabitants of Jerusalem.

crossReferences.connection: Hezekiah's Passover was compared to Solomon's; Josiah's exceeds even that, being compared to Samuel's time.

verses.text: This passover was kept in the eighteenth year of the reign of Josiah.
verses.explainer: This Passover is kept in the eighteenth year of Josiah's reign.

verses.text: After all this, when Josiah had prepared the temple, Neco king of Egypt went up to fight against Carchemish by the Euphrates, and Josiah went out against him.
verses.explainer: After all this, when Josiah has set the temple in order, Neco king of Egypt goes up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates, and Josiah goes out to confront him.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The Battle of Carchemish (605 BC) was one of antiquity's most significant battles, where Babylon defeated Egypt and Assyria, reshaping the entire Near Eastern world. Josiah's intervention was a geopolitical miscalculation with theological consequences.

verses.text: But he sent ambassadors to him, saying, “What have I to do with you, you king of Judah? I come not against you today, but against the house with which I have war. God has commanded me to make haste. Beware that it is God who is with me, that he not destroy you.”
verses.explainer: Neco sends messengers to Josiah saying: 'What have we to do with each other, king of Judah? I am not coming against you today but against the house with which I am at war, and God has commanded me to hurry. Cease opposing God, who is with me, lest He destroy you.'

thenVsNow.then: God sometimes speaks through unexpected sources -- even those outside the faith community. Josiah's refusal to hear God through an Egyptian pharaoh was a failure of discernment, not faith.
thenVsNow.now: God sometimes speaks through unexpected sources -- even those outside the faith community. Josiah's refusal to hear God through an Egyptian pharaoh was a failure of discernment, not faith.

verses.text: Nevertheless Josiah would not turn his face from him, but disguised himself, that he might fight with him, and didn’t listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God, and came to fight in the valley of Megiddo.
verses.explainer: But Josiah does not turn away from him, but disguises himself to fight against him. He does not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God, and comes to fight in the plain of Megiddo.

crossReferences.connection: Ahab also disguised himself in battle and died. Josiah's disguise echoes Ahab's fatal deception.

verses.text: The archers shot at king Josiah; and the king said to his servants, “Take me away, because I am seriously wounded!”
verses.explainer: The archers shoot King Josiah, and the king says to his servants, 'Take me away, for I am badly wounded.'

verses.text: So his servants took him out of the chariot, and put him in the second chariot that he had, and brought him to Jerusalem; and he died, and was buried in the tombs of his fathers. All Judah and Jerusalem mourned for Josiah.
verses.explainer: His servants take him out of the chariot, put him in his second chariot, and bring him to Jerusalem. He dies and is buried in the tombs of his fathers. All Judah and Jerusalem mourn for Josiah.

thenVsNow.then: Josiah's death at 39 cut short the most faithful reign since David. Sometimes the best leaders are taken early, and the community must face the future without them -- a painful but recurring reality.
thenVsNow.now: Josiah's death at 39 cut short the most faithful reign since David. Sometimes the best leaders are taken early, and the community must face the future without them -- a painful but recurring reality.

verses.text: Jeremiah lamented for Josiah, and all the singing men and singing women spoke of Josiah in their lamentations to this day; and they made them an ordinance in Israel. Behold, they are written in the lamentations.
verses.explainer: Jeremiah composes a lament for Josiah, and all the male and female singers speak of Josiah in their laments to this day. They make these a rule in Israel; they are written in the Laments.

crossReferences.connection: Jeremiah's later praise of Josiah: 'He judged the cause of the poor and needy; then it was well. Is not this to know me? declares the LORD.'

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Josiah, and his good deeds, according to that which is written in Yahweh’s law,
verses.explainer: The rest of Josiah's acts and his good deeds according to what is written in the Law of the LORD,

verses.text: and his acts, first and last, behold, they are written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah.
verses.explainer: and his acts from first to last are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Passover -- Josiah's Passover exceeded even Hezeki
wordStudies.meaning: Passover -- Josiah's Passover exceeded even Hezekiah's in scale and precision, becoming the standard against which all future celebrations would be measured.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To disguise oneself -- Josiah disguised himself in
wordStudies.meaning: To disguise oneself -- Josiah disguised himself in battle as Ahab had done (1 Kings 22:30). In both cases, disguise failed to protect against divine appointment -- a disguise cannot hide from God.

explainer.summary: The final chapter compresses four reigns and the destruction of Jerusalem into a rapid narrative. Jehoahaz reigns three months before being deposed by Egypt. Jehoiakim reigns eleven years doing evil and is carried off by Nebuchadnezzar. Jehoiachin reigns three months and ten days before deportation to Babylon. Zedekiah reigns eleven years, rebels against Babylon, and refuses to humble himself before Jeremiah. Jerusalem falls, the temple is burned, and the people are exiled. But the book ends not with destruction but with hope: Cyrus of Persia decrees that the temple be rebuilt.
themes: The destruction of temple and city
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 36 is the conclusion of the entire Chronicler's history. By ending with Cyrus's decree rather than the destruction of Jerusalem, the Chronicler frames the exile not as the end but as a pause before restoration. For the post-exilic community, this was the most important message: God's story continues.

verses.text: Then the people of the land took Jehoahaz the son of Josiah, and made him king in his father’s place in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The people of the land take Jehoahaz son of Josiah and make him king in Jerusalem in his father's place.

crossReferences.connection: The parallel account of Jehoahaz's brief reign after Josiah's death.

verses.text: Joahaz was twenty-three years old when he began to reign; and he reigned three months in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Jehoahaz is twenty-three when he becomes king, and he reigns three months in Jerusalem.

verses.text: The king of Egypt removed him from office at Jerusalem, and fined the land one hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold.
verses.explainer: The king of Egypt deposes him in Jerusalem and imposes on the land a tribute of a hundred talents of silver and a talent of gold.

verses.text: The king of Egypt made Eliakim his brother king over Judah and Jerusalem, and changed his name to Jehoiakim. Neco took Joahaz his brother, and carried him to Egypt.
verses.explainer: The king of Egypt makes Eliakim, Jehoahaz's brother, king over Judah and Jerusalem, changing his name to Jehoiakim. Neco takes his brother Jehoahaz to Egypt.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: Name changes by foreign powers signified vassalage. The original name Eliakim ('God raises up') was changed to Jehoiakim ('the LORD establishes') -- ironically, a more theologically rich name imposed by a pagan king.

verses.text: Jehoiakim was twenty-five years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. He did that which was evil in Yahweh his God’s sight.
verses.explainer: Jehoiakim is twenty-five when he becomes king, and he reigns eleven years in Jerusalem. He does evil in the sight of the LORD his God.

crossReferences.connection: Jeremiah's account of Jehoiakim burning the prophetic scroll -- one of the most defiant acts of any king.

verses.text: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came up against him, and bound him in fetters to carry him to Babylon.
verses.explainer: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon comes up against him and binds him in chains to take him to Babylon.

verses.text: Nebuchadnezzar also carried some of the vessels of Yahweh’s house to Babylon, and put them in his temple at Babylon.
verses.explainer: Nebuchadnezzar also carries some of the vessels of the house of the LORD to Babylon and puts them in his temple in Babylon.

crossReferences.connection: Cyrus returns these same temple vessels, proving that Babylon could possess them temporarily but never permanently.

verses.text: Now the rest of the acts of Jehoiakim, and his abominations which he did, and that which was found in him, behold, they are written in the book of the kings of Israel and Judah; and Jehoiachin his son reigned in his place.
verses.explainer: The rest of Jehoiakim's acts, the abominations he does, and what is found against him are written in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah. His son Jehoiachin reigns in his place.

verses.text: Jehoiachin was eight years old when he began to reign, and he reigned three months and ten days in Jerusalem. He did that which was evil in Yahweh’s sight.
verses.explainer: Jehoiachin is eighteen years old when he becomes king, and he reigns three months and ten days in Jerusalem. He does evil in the sight of the LORD.

crossReferences.connection: The Kings account says Jehoiachin was eighteen (some manuscripts say eight); Chronicles says eighteen.

verses.text: At the return of the year, king Nebuchadnezzar sent and brought him to Babylon, with the valuable vessels of Yahweh’s house, and made Zedekiah his brother king over Judah and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: In the spring, King Nebuchadnezzar sends and brings him to Babylon, along with the precious vessels of the house of the LORD, and makes his kinsman Zedekiah king over Judah and Jerusalem.

thenVsNow.then: The progressive loss of temple treasures -- some under Jehoiakim, more under Jehoiachin, all under Zedekiah -- mirrors how institutional decline often happens gradually, with each stage making the next more devastating.
thenVsNow.now: The progressive loss of temple treasures -- some under Jehoiakim, more under Jehoiachin, all under Zedekiah -- mirrors how institutional decline often happens gradually, with each stage making the next more devastating.

verses.text: Zedekiah was twenty-one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Zedekiah is twenty-one when he becomes king, and he reigns eleven years in Jerusalem.

verses.text: He did that which was evil in Yahweh his God’s sight. He didn’t humble himself before Jeremiah the prophet speaking from Yahweh’s mouth.
verses.explainer: He does evil in the sight of the LORD his God. He does not humble himself before Jeremiah the prophet, who speaks from the mouth of the LORD.

crossReferences.connection: Jeremiah's final appeal to Zedekiah to surrender to Babylon, which Zedekiah refuses out of fear.

verses.text: He also rebelled against king Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear by God; but he stiffened his neck, and hardened his heart against turning to Yahweh, the God of Israel.
verses.explainer: He also rebels against King Nebuchadnezzar, who had made him swear by God. He stiffens his neck and hardens his heart against turning to the LORD God of Israel.

verses.text: Moreover all the chiefs of the priests, and the people, trespassed very greatly after all the abominations of the nations; and they polluted Yahweh’s house which he had made holy in Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: All the officers of the priests and the people likewise are exceedingly unfaithful, following all the abominations of the nations. They defile the house of the LORD that He has consecrated in Jerusalem.

verses.text: Yahweh, the God of their fathers, sent to them by his messengers, rising up early and sending, because he had compassion on his people, and on his dwelling place;
verses.explainer: The LORD, the God of their fathers, sends word to them persistently by His messengers, because He has compassion on His people and on His dwelling place.

thenVsNow.then: God's patience in sending 'messenger after messenger' before judgment reveals a divine character that exhausts every avenue of mercy before allowing consequences. This patience defines God's nature throughout Scripture.
thenVsNow.now: God's patience in sending 'messenger after messenger' before judgment reveals a divine character that exhausts every avenue of mercy before allowing consequences. This patience defines God's nature throughout Scripture.

verses.text: but they mocked the messengers of God, and despised his words, and scoffed at his prophets, until Yahweh’s wrath arose against his people, until there was no remedy.
verses.explainer: But they keep mocking the messengers of God, despising His words, and scoffing at His prophets, until the wrath of the LORD rises against His people, until there is no remedy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The phrase 'no remedy' echoes medical language -- the national sickness of idolatry and prophetic rejection had progressed beyond treatment. This is the theological explanation for why even Josiah's reform could not prevent the exile.

verses.text: Therefore he brought on them the king of the Chaldeans, who killed their young men with the sword in the house of their sanctuary, and had no compassion on young man or virgin, old man or gray-headed. He gave them all into his hand.
verses.explainer: Therefore He brings against them the king of the Chaldeans, who kills their young men with the sword in the house of their sanctuary and has no compassion on young man or virgin, old man or aged. God gives them all into his hand.

annotations.word: in the house of their sanctuary

verses.text: All the vessels of God’s house, great and small, and the treasures of Yahweh’s house, and the treasures of the king, and of his princes, all these he brought to Babylon.
verses.explainer: All the vessels of the house of God, great and small, the treasures of the house of the LORD, the treasures of the king and his officials -- all these he brings to Babylon.

thenVsNow.then: The complete stripping of the temple -- every last vessel and treasure taken -- represents the total loss of institutional religion. For the post-exilic community, this was not just history but personal memory.
thenVsNow.now: The complete stripping of the temple -- every last vessel and treasure taken -- represents the total loss of institutional religion. For the post-exilic community, this was not just history but personal memory.

verses.text: They burned God’s house, and broke down the wall of Jerusalem, and burned all its palaces with fire, and destroyed all of its valuable vessels.
verses.explainer: They burn the house of God, break down the wall of Jerusalem, burn all its palaces with fire, and destroy all its precious vessels.

crossReferences.connection: God's promise that His eyes and heart would be in the temple 'perpetually' -- the destruction raises the question of whether the promise has failed.

verses.text: He carried those who had escaped from the sword away to Babylon, and they were servants to him and his sons until the reign of the kingdom of Persia,
verses.explainer: He takes into exile in Babylon those who escape the sword, and they become servants to him and his sons until the kingdom of Persia rises to power.

verses.text: to fulfill Yahweh’s word by Jeremiah’s mouth, until the land had enjoyed its Sabbaths. As long as it lay desolate, it kept Sabbath, to fulfill seventy years.
verses.explainer: This fulfills the word of the LORD by Jeremiah, until the land has enjoyed its Sabbaths. All the days it lay desolate it kept Sabbath, to fulfill seventy years.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: The 'seventy years' may represent the roughly 490 years of the monarchy during which Sabbath years were neglected (490/7 = 70). The exile was cosmic accounting -- the land collecting its overdue rest.

crossReferences.connection: The Sabbath year laws requiring the land to rest every seventh year -- laws Israel had ignored, resulting in the exile's duration.
Jeremiah's prophecy of seventy years of exile that this verse explicitly cites.

verses.text: Now in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that Yahweh’s word by the mouth of Jeremiah might be accomplished, Yahweh stirred up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, so that he made a proclamation throughout all his kingdom, and put it also in writing, saying,
verses.explainer: In the first year of Cyrus king of Persia, that the word of the LORD by Jeremiah might be fulfilled, the LORD stirs up the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia so that he makes a proclamation throughout all his kingdom and puts it in writing:

crossReferences.connection: Isaiah prophesied Cyrus by name as God's 'shepherd' and 'anointed' who would authorize the temple's rebuilding -- decades before Cyrus rose to power.
Ezra opens with this same decree, continuing the narrative where 2 Chronicles ends.

thenVsNow.then: That the Bible's historical narrative ends not with destruction but with a pagan king's decree of restoration sets the pattern for all of Scripture: the last word is never judgment but hope.
thenVsNow.now: That the Bible's historical narrative ends not with destruction but with a pagan king's decree of restoration sets the pattern for all of Scripture: the last word is never judgment but hope.

verses.text: “Thus says Cyrus king of Persia, ‘Yahweh, the God of heaven, has given all the kingdoms of the earth to me; and he has commanded me to build him a house in Jerusalem, which is in Judah. Whoever there is among you of all his people, Yahweh his God be with him, and let him go up.’”
verses.explainer: 'Thus says Cyrus king of Persia: The LORD, the God of heaven, has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and He has charged me to build Him a house at Jerusalem, which is in Judah. Whoever is among you of all His people, may the LORD his God be with him. Let him go up.'

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: In the Hebrew Bible's traditional ordering, 2 Chronicles is the final book. This means the entire Old Testament ends with 'let him go up' -- an invitation to return, rebuild, and begin again. The post-exilic community heard this as God's last word: come home.

thenVsNow.then: The final word of 2 Chronicles -- 'let him go up' -- is an invitation, not a conclusion. History's darkest chapters end with open doors. Exile is never the final word; restoration is always possible.
thenVsNow.now: The final word of 2 Chronicles -- 'let him go up' -- is an invitation, not a conclusion. History's darkest chapters end with open doors. Exile is never the final word; restoration is always possible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Remedy, healing, cure -- when the Chronicler says
wordStudies.meaning: Remedy, healing, cure -- when the Chronicler says there was 'no remedy' for Judah's condition, this medical metaphor indicates that the national sickness had progressed beyond treatment. Judgment was the only remaining option.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Sabbath, rest -- the land 'kept Sabbath' during th
wordStudies.meaning: Sabbath, rest -- the land 'kept Sabbath' during the exile, receiving the rest Israel had denied it. The word connects creation rest (Genesis 2:2-3), weekly rest, and the land's enforced rest during exile.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To go up, ascend -- the final word of 2 Chronicles
wordStudies.meaning: To go up, ascend -- the final word of 2 Chronicles ('let him go up') carries both physical meaning (ascending to Jerusalem's elevation) and spiritual meaning (rising to meet God). It is an invitation, not a command.
